title: Ezekiel 48
subtitle: The Tribal Allotments and YHWH Shammah

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — New Temple Vision

heb.paragraph: Each tribe receives a cheleq (portion), a horizontal strip of land running east-west across the full width of the country from the eastern border to the Mediterranean. This egalitarian arrangement — equal width for each tribe — replaces the irregular, size-based allocations of Joshua's distribution. Seven tribes are north of the sacred district (Dan, Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Reuben, Judah) and five are south (Benjamin, Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun, Gad). Judah is placed immediately north of the sacred district, the position of greatest honor.

refs.note: The original tribal land distribution under Joshua. Ezekiel's egalitarian strips replace Joshua's irregular and contested boundaries.
Jacob's blessing of the twelve tribes. Ezekiel's tribal arrangement partially reflects but significantly reorganizes the traditional tribal order.
The 144,000 from the twelve tribes of Israel. Revelation's tribal list also rearranges the traditional order, omitting Dan entirely — possibly reflecting Dan's association with idolatry.

notes.note: Dan is placed first, at the northern extremity. Despite Dan's historic association with idolatry (Judg 18:30-31), the tribe is included in the eschatological allotment. God's restoration extends even to the tribe that strayed furthest. However, Dan's placement at the border may reflect a lower position of honor compared to tribes closer to the sacred center.

Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, and Reuben occupy the northern positions. The equal-width strips are a radical departure from historical allocation, where some tribes received far more territory than others. In God's new order, equity replaces the inequalities that generated inter-tribal conflict.

Judah receives the strip immediately north of the sacred district, the most honored position. As the tribe of David and the messianic line, Judah's proximity to the temple reflects its unique role in redemptive history. Benjamin, David's ally, is placed immediately south of the district (v. 23).

The land distribution is by divine fiat, not by conquest, lot, or negotiation. YHWH alone determines who lives where. The sovereignty of God in allocation eliminates human competition and establishes a divinely ordered geography.

Calvin reads the equal tribal allotments as a figure for the equality of all believers before God. In the church, no member has greater standing than another. The placement of tribes near or far from the sacred center represents degrees of spiritual maturity, not of inherent worth. Calvin warns against drawing literal maps from visionary geography.

The NET notes that Dan's allotment follows the northern border described in 47:15-17. The phrase 'one portion for Dan' (Dan echad) uses the Hebrew word echad (one), which becomes a formulaic marker throughout the chapter for each tribe's equal share.

The NET observes that Judah's position 'beside the border of Benjamin' in v. 22 and 'beside the territory of the sacred district' reflects Judah's historical association with the temple in Jerusalem. The tribe whose capital housed the temple is placed closest to the eschatological temple.

Block identifies the tribal arrangement as a 'theological geography' that prioritizes proximity to the sacred center over historical precedent. The seven-five split (seven north, five south) places the sacred district slightly south of center, roughly corresponding to Jerusalem's actual location. Block notes that the equal-width strips eliminate the source of inter-tribal boundary disputes that plagued Israel throughout the settlement and monarchic periods.

Zimmerli classifies this section as Stammesliste (tribal list) and notes its departure from all known historical tribal geographies. The egalitarian strips represent an idealized redistribution that owes more to theological principle than to topographical reality. Zimmerli observes that the ordering places Rachel tribes (Ephraim, Manasseh, Benjamin) closest to the sacred center, possibly reflecting a tradition that privileges the Joseph/Rachel lineage.

hist.context: The tribal allotments begin with the seven northern tribes, listed from north to south: Dan, Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Reuben, and Judah. Each tribe receives an east-west strip of equal width spanning the entire breadth of the land. This arrangement is strikingly different from the historical distribution under Joshua, where tribal territories varied greatly in size and shape. The egalitarian design reflects the principle that all tribes are equal before YHWH. The ordering departs from both birth order and historical precedent. Dan, the northernmost, was historically the tribe most associated with idolatry (the Dan sanctuary of Judges 18). His placement at the extreme north may represent a restoration from the margins. Judah is positioned immediately north of the sacred district — the most prestigious location — reflecting Judah's role as the royal and messianic tribe. The sacred district itself (detailed in vv. 8-22) occupies the center of the land, making the temple the literal and figurative heart of the nation.

sections.header: Verses 8-22 — The Sacred District and the City

heb.paragraph: The miqdash (sanctuary) occupies the exact center of the sacred district, which itself occupies the exact center of the land. This concentric positioning establishes the temple as the geographic, political, and theological nucleus of the nation. The sacred district (terumah) measures 25,000 by 25,000 cubits (approximately 13 km square) and is divided into three horizontal strips: the priestly zone (25,000 x 10,000), the Levitical zone (25,000 x 10,000), and the city zone (25,000 x 5,000). The prince's territory flanks the entire district east and west.

refs.note: The initial description of the sacred district. Chapter 48 provides the same information in greater detail and within the context of the full tribal distribution.
The New Jerusalem has twelve gates, three on each side, named for the twelve tribes of Israel. This directly echoes Ezekiel 48:30-35.
The Israelite camp in the wilderness was arranged with the tabernacle at the center and the tribes in concentric rings. Ezekiel's sacred district follows the same principle of centralized holiness.

notes.note: The sanctuary sits at the center of the priestly zone, which sits at the center of the sacred district, which sits at the center of the nation. Concentric holiness radiates outward from God's dwelling. Every aspect of national geography is oriented toward the temple.

The Levitical territory is parallel to and equal in size to the priestly zone. The Levites may not sell, exchange, or alienate their land because it is holy to the LORD. The inalienability of sacred land prevents the economic pressures that led to land consolidation in the pre-exilic period.

The city belongs to all Israel, not to any single tribe. Workers from all twelve tribes cultivate its agricultural land. The capital city is deliberately pan-Israelite, preventing any tribe from claiming exclusive ownership of the nation's center.

The prince's territory flanks the sacred district on both sides. His land is clearly bounded, preventing encroachment on sacred or tribal property. The architectural separation of political and sacred power is the spatial expression of constitutional restraint.

Calvin interprets the sacred district as representing the church's central place in God's purposes. The concentric arrangement — sanctuary, priests, Levites, city, prince — illustrates the ordering of spiritual priorities. God's presence is at the center; all other authority serves and surrounds it. The city belonging to all tribes represents the catholic (universal) nature of the church, to which all peoples belong.

The NET notes that the priestly zone 'shall be most holy' (qodesh qodashim), applying the designation normally reserved for the inner sanctuary to the entire priestly territory. This expansion of the highest sanctity classification to a residential zone is unprecedented.

The NET observes that the city zone is explicitly designated 'common' (chol), distinguishing it from the 'most holy' priestly zone and the 'holy' Levitical zone. The three-tier sanctity classification (most holy, holy, common) governs the entire district's spatial organization.

Block describes this passage as the 'constitutional geography' of the new Israel, establishing in spatial terms the relationships between YHWH, priests, Levites, the city, the people, and the prince. The concentric design is not merely aesthetic but theological: proximity to the sanctuary correlates with sanctity, and the boundaries between zones are inviolable. Block emphasizes that the prince's flanking position is deliberately non-central: political authority surrounds but does not control sacred space.

Zimmerli regards this section as a Heiligtumsordnung (sanctuary ordinance) that recapitulates 45:1-8 with greater precision. He notes that the three-tier sanctity classification (most holy, holy, common) maps the priestly holiness spectrum onto territorial geography, creating what he calls a 'geography of holiness' that extends the temple's spatial logic to the entire national territory.

hist.context: The sacred district described in 45:1-8 is now elaborated in full detail within the context of the tribal allotments. The district is a square of 25,000 by 25,000 cubits, centered between the tribal territories of Judah (north) and Benjamin (south). The northern half (25,000 x 10,000) belongs to the Zadokite priests and contains the sanctuary at its center. Below it, a parallel strip (25,000 x 10,000) is assigned to the Levites. The southern strip (25,000 x 5,000) is the city's property, containing the city of 4,500 cubits per side with an open area of 250 cubits on each side for grazing and cultivation. The city has twelve gates, three on each side, each named for a tribe of Israel. Workers from all tribes cultivate the city's land, making the capital a truly pan-Israelite entity. The prince's territory extends east and west from the sacred district to the national borders, flanking the terumah on both sides. His land is bounded by the sacred district, so that he cannot encroach on holy space.

sections.header: Verses 23-35 — The Southern Tribes and the City Gates: YHWH Shammah

heb.paragraph: The final two words of the book — YHWH Shammah ('The LORD is There') — constitute the theological capstone of the entire prophecy. The city's name is not Jerusalem but YHWH Shammah, because the permanent, unmediated presence of God defines its identity. This name answers the crisis that launched the book: YHWH's glory departed (chs. 10-11), and now YHWH is there. The name forms an inclusio with the opening vision by the Kebar Canal (1:1-3), where Ezekiel encountered the mobile glory in exile. The glory that was mobile has become fixed; the God who departed has returned to stay.

The city has twelve she'arim (gates), three per side, each named for a tribe: north (Reuben, Judah, Levi), east (Joseph, Benjamin, Dan), south (Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun), west (Gad, Asher, Naphtali). The inclusion of Levi and Joseph (not Ephraim/Manasseh separately) yields exactly twelve names. The gate-naming ensures every tribe has a permanent stake in the holy city, regardless of where their territorial allotment lies.

refs.note: The glory departed from the city. The name YHWH Shammah declares the reversal: God is now permanently there.
The city will be called 'The City of the LORD, the Zion of the Holy One of Israel.' The renaming tradition for the eschatological city appears in multiple prophetic texts.
Jerusalem will be called 'The LORD Our Righteousness.' Like Ezekiel's YHWH Shammah, the city's new name expresses a theological truth about God's character and presence.
The New Jerusalem comes down from heaven, and a voice declares: 'God's dwelling place is now among the people.' Revelation's vision of ultimate divine presence fulfills Ezekiel's YHWH Shammah.
The New Jerusalem has twelve gates, three on each side, each inscribed with the name of a tribe of Israel — directly drawn from Ezekiel 48:30-35.

notes.note: The five southern tribes complete the territorial distribution. Benjamin's placement next to the sacred district, mirroring Judah's position on the north, reflects the Judah-Benjamin alliance that constituted the southern kingdom. In God's restored order, this alliance is honored with proximity to the temple.

The southern boundary of Gad is also the southern boundary of the nation, extending from Tamar to the waters of Meribah-Kadesh to the Wadi of Egypt to the Mediterranean. The same boundary described in 47:19, now anchored to a specific tribal territory.

The twelve gates ensure that every tribe has permanent access to the holy city. Levi receives a gate despite having no territorial allotment, recognizing their unique role as the priestly tribe. The gate arrangement differs from the tribal ordering of the allotments, suggesting an independent tradition about the city's design.

YHWH Shammah — The LORD is There. These are the last words of Ezekiel and the theological conclusion of the entire book. The crisis of chapters 8-11 was divine departure; the resolution of chapters 40-48 is divine return; and the final word is divine permanence. God is there, and He will never leave.

The circumference of the city is 18,000 cubits. The total of the twelve named gates encompassing the city means that every approach to God's dwelling is marked by tribal identity. Israel's corporate identity surrounds the city of God's presence.

Calvin sees the southern tribal allotments as completing the vision of a perfectly ordered society in which every member has a secure inheritance. The security of land tenure represents the security of salvation: what God has given, no one can take away.

Calvin interprets the twelve gates as representing the openness of God's kingdom to all who come through proper channels. The gates are always named — there is no anonymous entry. Every member of God's people is known and counted. The name YHWH Shammah is the ultimate promise: God's presence, not human achievement, defines the city. Calvin applies this to the church: what makes any congregation holy is not its buildings, traditions, or leaders, but the presence of God in its midst.

The NET notes that the gate-naming includes Levi (north side) and counts Joseph as one (east side), yielding exactly twelve names. This differs from both the territorial allotment (where Levi has no land and Joseph is split into Ephraim and Manasseh) and from Revelation 7's tribal list (which omits Dan). Each list arranges the tribes according to its own theological purposes.

The NET observes that 'YHWH Shammah' is not a proper noun functioning as a replacement for 'Jerusalem' but a theological epithet: 'The LORD is there.' The city retains its identity; its new name expresses its defining characteristic. The presence (shamah, 'there') corresponds to the noun Shekhinah in later Jewish theology, though the term itself does not occur in the Hebrew Bible.

Block notes that the southern tribes are listed more briefly than the northern tribes, without the border descriptions that accompanied the northern list. This literary compression suggests that the author considers the detailed northern description sufficient to establish the pattern for the south.

Block calls YHWH Shammah 'the most important words in the book of Ezekiel.' The entire forty-eight-chapter arc — from the glory's appearance by the Kebar Canal (1:1-3) through its departure from the temple (10-11) through the long middle section of judgment and restoration (12-39) through the temple vision (40-48) — culminates in this two-word declaration. Block emphasizes that the city is not named for David, or Zion, or Jerusalem, but for the presence of God. The name is the thesis: God's presence is what makes a city holy, a people chosen, and a future possible.

Zimmerli identifies YHWH Shammah as the Schlussformel (concluding formula) of the entire book. He notes that the twelve-gate city echoes both the wilderness camp arrangement (Num 2) and the eschatological city of later apocalyptic tradition (Rev 21). The two-word name accomplishes theologically what forty-eight chapters have narrated dramatically: the restored, permanent presence of YHWH among His people. For Zimmerli, this is the Grundaussage (foundational statement) of Ezekiel's theology: God's presence, lost through Israel's sin, has been recovered through divine initiative alone.

hist.context: The five southern tribes are listed from north to south: Benjamin, Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun, and Gad. Benjamin, placed immediately south of the sacred district, mirrors Judah's honored position to the north, reflecting the historical alliance between Judah and Benjamin. The chapter concludes with the city's dimensions (4,500 cubits per side, approximately 2.4 km) and its twelve gates, three per wall, each named for a tribe. The gate-naming includes Levi (who receives no territorial allotment but has a gate) and counts Joseph as one tribe (rather than splitting Ephraim and Manasseh), yielding exactly twelve gates. The final verse delivers the climactic revelation: the name of the city from that day forward shall be YHWH Shammah — 'The LORD is There.' This closing declaration resolves the central tension of the book. The glory departed (10:18-19; 11:22-23), leaving the temple and city empty of divine presence. Through forty-eight chapters of judgment, exile, and visionary restoration, the book has led to this culminating word: God is permanently present with His people. The God who left has returned, and His return is permanent.

rows.label: vv.8-12
rows.text: Sacred District: Priestly Zone and Sanctuary

rows.label: vv.30-34
rows.text: Twelve Gates Named for Twelve Tribes

rows.label: vv.35
rows.text: YHWH Shammah: The LORD is There

lit.note: Chapter 48 brings the book to its climactic conclusion. The tribal allotments establish an egalitarian sacred geography; the twelve gates ensure universal tribal access; and the final name — YHWH Shammah — resolves the entire book's central crisis: God, who departed, is permanently present.

themes.note: Covenant and Sovereignty peak as the chapter fulfills the patriarchal land promise through divine allocation. Prophecy scores high as the entire temple vision reaches its prophetic climax in YHWH Shammah. Holiness remains prominent through the sacred district's sanctity gradations. Mercy is elevated by the final declaration of permanent divine presence after the judgment of exile.

hebtext.note: Each tribe receives a cheleq (portion), a horizontal strip of land running east-west across the full width of the country from the eastern border to the Mediterranean. This egalitarian arrangement — equal width for each tribe — replaces the irregular, size-based allocations of Joshua's distribution. Seven tribes are north of the sacred district (Dan, Asher, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Reuben, Judah) and five are south (Benjamin, Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun, Gad). Judah is placed immediately north of the sacred district, the position of greatest honor.

The miqdash (sanctuary) occupies the exact center of the sacred district, which itself occupies the exact center of the land. This concentric positioning establishes the temple as the geographic, political, and theological nucleus of the nation. The sacred district (terumah) measures 25,000 by 25,000 cubits (approximately 13 km square) and is divided into three horizontal strips: the priestly zone (25,000 x 10,000), the Levitical zone (25,000 x 10,000), and the city zone (25,000 x 5,000). The prince's territory flanks the entire district east and west.

The final two words of the book — YHWH Shammah ('The LORD is There') — constitute the theological capstone of the entire prophecy. The city's name is not Jerusalem but YHWH Shammah, because the permanent, unmediated presence of God defines its identity. This name answers the crisis that launched the book: YHWH's glory departed (chs. 10-11), and now YHWH is there. The name forms an inclusio with the opening vision by the Kebar Canal (1:1-3), where Ezekiel encountered the mobile glory in exile. The glory that was mobile has become fixed; the God who departed has returned to stay.

The city has twelve she'arim (gates), three per side, each named for a tribe: north (Reuben, Judah, Levi), east (Joseph, Benjamin, Dan), south (Simeon, Issachar, Zebulun), west (Gad, Asher, Naphtali). The inclusion of Levi and Joseph (not Ephraim/Manasseh separately) yields exactly twelve names. The gate-naming ensures every tribe has a permanent stake in the holy city, regardless of where their territorial allotment lies.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: These are the last words of Ezekiel and the theological conclusion of the entire book…

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: As the tribe of David and the messianic line, Judah's proximity to the temple reflects its unique role in redemptive history…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Tribal Allotments and YHWH Shammah

translations.text: “Bordering the territory of Judah from east to west will be the portion you are to present as a special gift. It will be 25,000 cubits wide, and its length from east to west will equal one of the tribal portions; the sanctuary will be in the center of it.

“Adjoining the territory of Judah, from the east side to the west, shall be the portion which you shall set apart, 25,000 cubits in breadth, and in length equal to one of the tribal portions, from the east side to the west, with the sanctuary in the midst of it.
“The special portion you are to offer to the Lord will be 25,000 cubits long and 10,000 cubits wide.
The portion that you shall set apart for the LORD shall be 25,000 cubits in length, and 20,000 in breadth.
